segments.s156-g9: همه سیستان پیش باز آمدند is a simple past clause: همه سیستان is the subject ('all of Sistan'); پیش باز آمدند is a verbal phrase meaning 'came forward again'. باز is the adverb 'again' modifying آمدند; پیش here has the sense 'forward / in front (came forward)'. Word order is subject + adverbial/verb, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s156-g10: به رنج و به درد و گداز آمدند is a coordinated prepositional/ adverbial phrase that describes manner: به + noun = 'with / in (suffering)'. The repeated به before each noun is a rhetorical (poetic) emphatic pattern. گداز here is a poetic noun meaning 'ardor/anguish' and آمدند repeats the past plural verb 'they came', echoing the motion begun in the previous segment.
segments.s156-g11: چو تابوت را دید introduces a temporal clause with چو = 'when'; تابوت را is the definite object (را marks definiteness/object), and دید is the 3rd-person past 'saw' or 'was seen' depending on subject. دستان سام follows the verb: دستان here is the syntactic subject phrase attached to سام (literally 'Sam's hands' but functioning metonymically/poetically as Sam himself). The sequence reflects classic Persian inversion where the temporal clause + verb precede the fuller subject phrase.
segments.s156-g12: فرود آمد از اسپ زرین ستام is a compound verbal idea: فرود (alighting/descent) + آمد (past auxiliary) = 'alighted / came down'. از + اسپ زرین specifies the source: 'from the golden steed'. زرین is the adjective 'golden' following the noun (normal in Persian poetry). ستام here follows as a name or attributive element attached to the horse phrase.
segments.s156-g13: تهمتن پیاده همی رفت پیش is a simple past action with an adverbial and progressive particle: تهمتن is the subject (name), پیاده 'on foot' is an adverbial phrase, همی is an archaic/prosodic particle used in Ferdowsi that marks ongoing or iterative action, رفت is the past 'went', and پیش means 'forward / ahead'. Together: 'Tahmatan went forward on foot.'
segments.s156-g14: دریده همه جامه دل کرده ریش strings together participial and verblike modifiers describing his appearance and state. دریده (a past participle) modifies جامه: 'garments torn'; همه جامه = 'all the garments' (whole of his clothes). دل کرده is a compound where کرده (past participle of کردن) makes دل 'heart' into a predicate meaning 'heart-stricken / grieved'. ریش (beard) is appended as a final image (understood as disordered or torn) — this compact listing of images is typical of epic diction.
segments.s156-g15: گشادند گردان سراسر کمر is a nominal-verb clause: گردان (troops/companies) is the subject and گشادند (literally 'opened' or 'spread') is the past plural verb meaning 'they opened/parted'. سراسر کمر is an adverbial phrase 'throughout the waist/line'—kamar here is used figuratively for the line or flank—so the phrase means 'the ranks opened along the whole line.'
segments.s156-g16: همه پیش تابوت بر خاک سر is a locative/resultative clause describing the people's action: همه 'all' is subject, پیش تابوت 'before the coffin' marks location, بر خاک سر is an idiomatic phrase 'with heads to the ground' (بر + خاک = 'on the ground'; سر = 'head' indicates they prostrate/bowed their heads). The compact ordering omits an explicit verb like 'bowed' but the sense is clear from the context and epic style.
segments.s156-g33: تو گفتی که introduces a reported clause: تو گفتی (you said) + که (that) marks the following clause as the content. سام است places the proper name before the copula (name + است = 'Sam is/was'); the phrase با یال و سفت is a post‑nominal adjunct ('with [his] mane and [his] strength/firmness') modifying سام. Word order is typical classical Persian: subject/name before the verb 'است'.
segments.s156-g34: غمی شد uses شد as a change/auxiliary meaning 'became' or 'was stricken' so 'he became sorrowful'; ز جنگ shows cause ('from/because of the war'); اندر آمد بخفت is a sequence: اندر آمد ('he came in/entered') followed by بخفت (past verb 'slept' — the prefixed ب‑ is an older/poetic verbal form). The two verbs are narrative sequence: 'became sorrowful, entered, and slept.'
segments.s156-g35: بپوشید is a past transitive verb 'he covered/wrapped (him)'; بازش is باز + the clitic -ش (him), so 'again him' = 'him again' (i.e., 'he wrapped him again'). به دیبای زرد is a prepositional phrase 'in/with yellow brocade' specifying the material. The implied subject is third person singular (he).
segments.s156-g36: سر تنگ تابوت is a genitive/compound phrase 'the coffin's narrow/top lid' (سر = top/head, تنگ = narrow); را is the definite object marker attaching to the whole NP; سخت کرد is a causative/past construction 'made firm' or 'sealed tightly' (کرد = did/made, سخت = firmly/tightly). The whole means he firmly closed/sealed the coffin's lid.
segments.s156-g37: همی گفت is the iterative/progressive past 'he kept saying' (همی marks ongoing or habitual action in classical Persian). اگر دخمه زرین کنم is a conditional clause: اگر (if) + [دخمه زرین] (golden tomb) + کنم (subjunctive/present‑tense 'I make' used here for the protasis). The speaker is expressing a hypothetical plan.
segments.s156-g38: ز مشک سیه گردش آگین کنم continues the conditional apodosis: ز (poetic 'از/with') + مشک سیه ('black musk') marks the material; گردش here means 'a circuit/band/encircling' (i.e., an ornamental band) and آگین means 'adorned/bright' — followed by کنم (I will make). Together: 'I will encircle/ornament it with a band of black musk / make it shining.' Word order places the instrumental phrase before the verb, as is normal.
segments.s156-g39: چو من رفته باشم is a temporal clause 'when I have gone' (چو = when; رفته باشم is a perfect/subjunctive-like compound meaning 'I am gone/have gone'). نماند بجای uses نماند (there will not remain) + بجای ('in [my] place') to say 'no one will remain in my stead.' The negative sense comes from نماند (not remain).
segments.s156-g40: وگرنه ('otherwise') begins a contrasting clause. مرا را (here shown as م + را) + خود emphasize 'for me myself' and جزین 'except this' with نیست رای ('there is no counsel/opinion') yields 'for me there is no counsel/opinion except this' — i.e. 'I myself have no other opinion besides this.' This is classical inversive word order where مرا/خود are emphatic and نیست follows to negate alternative counsel.
segments.s156-g41: The clause is inverted: 'یکی' (an indefinite subject 'someone') + object 'دخمه' (a tomb) and the verb 'کردش' (کرد + ـش, 'made him/it') follow. 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from', and the noun phrase 'سم ستور' literally 'the hoof of the steed(s)' is the material/source of the tomb. In older/poetic Persian the object pronoun is suffixed to the verb (کردش = 'made him/it').
segments.s156-g42: 'جهانی' (the world/as a whole) is the subject, followed by the prepositional phrase 'ز زاری' ('from/through lamentation') giving the cause. 'همی' is the archaic/progressive particle marking continuous or habitual aspect, here supporting the past progressive 'همی گشت' ('was becoming' / 'grew'), and 'کور' is the predicative adjective 'blind'. The clause therefore reads 'the whole world, from lamentation, became blind.'
segments.s156-g43: 'چنین' ('thus') opens the clause. 'گفت' here is the past-tense verb 'said/spoke' (standard poetic past), with 'بهرام' the agent/name. 'نیکو سخن' is an adjectival phrase 'good/well (spoken) speech' describing how he spoke; adjective + noun order is normal here. Note: the provided senses list for 'گفت' does not include the verbal sense, so sense index is set to -1 for that word.
segments.s156-g44: The relative/conjunction 'که' introduces the content of what was said. 'با' ('with') combines with 'مردگان' ('the dead'), and 'آشنایی' ('acquaintance/familiarity') is the object of the verb phrase. 'مکن' is the negative imperative ('do not'); together 'که با مردگان آشنایی مکن' = 'that/so: do not make acquaintance with the dead' (i.e., 'do not become familiar with the dead').
segments.s156-g45: This clause begins with the prohibitive 'نه' ('do not') applying to 'ایدر همی ماند' ('remain here' with 'همی' marking continuous/habitual aspect). The conditional 'خواهی دراز' is elliptical: 'خواهی' = 'if you wish' and 'دراز' = 'long' (understood as 'long‑lived' or 'to live long'). Together: 'Do not stay here; if you want to live long...' The conditional is typical in older Persian where the consequence clause follows.
segments.s156-g46: 'بسیچیده' is a past‑participle/adjectival form meaning 'well‑arranged' or 'well gathered' and is being used predicatively with the imperative 'باش' ('be'). The coordinating 'و' links the second command, where 'درنگی' is a noun 'a pause/delay' and 'مساز' is the prohibitive imperative 'do not make.' So: 'Be well prepared and do not make a pause/delay.'
segments.s156-g47: Word order here is inverted for poetic effect: 'به تو' (to you, dative) precedes the verb 'داد' (gave), while 'یک روز' ('one day') is a temporal adverbial and 'نوبت پدر' ('the father's turn') is the object. Literally: 'One day the father's turn gave to you' — i.e. 'one day your father's turn will be given to you' (you will take your father's place).
segments.s156-g48: 'سزد‌گر' here functions as a conditional concessive ('if/when it is fitting' or 'should it befit'). The pronoun 'ت' together with the object marker 'را' form 'ترا' ('to you'). 'نوبت آید بسر' is an idiomatic verbal phrase: 'نوبت آید' ('the turn comes') + 'بسر' (here indicating 'to befall/spend/come upon'). So the clause reads 'If it befit, when your turn comes upon you...' with an implied conclusion (endure/accept it).
segments.s156-g25: ازو is the old/poetic pronoun phrase 'از + او' meaning 'from it/him' and marks the source. میخ (nail) is the direct object of برکند, a past-tense (narrative) verb meaning 'tore out / dislodged'. The conjunction و links the next verb phrase; بگشاد is a past narrative form built with the verbal prefix ب- plus the root گشاد (here 'open') meaning 'opened', and سر here means 'lid/top' (the top of the coffin) not a person’s head.
segments.s156-g26: کفن is the noun 'shroud' and is the theme of the following action. ز (poetic form of از) means 'from' (i.e. 'from him/it'), and جدا کرد is a two-word verbal idea: جدا (apart) + کرد (did/made) — together 'detached / separated'. پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before/in the presence of his father' modifying the action.
segments.s156-g27: تنش را is the definite object 'his body' marked by را. بدَان نامداران functions as a recipient phrase: ب is the preposition 'to', and دان here archaically forms 'those/that' so بدان نامداران = 'to those renowned ones'. نمود is the past narrative of کردن/نمودن meaning 'showed / displayed'.
segments.s156-g28: تو گفتی literally reads 'you said' (تو = 'you'); here it introduces a reported clause. که is the subordinating particle 'that'. از چرخ برخاست دود has normal word order 'smoke (دود) rose (برخاست) from the sky (از چرخ)' — چرخ = 'sky/heaven' and برخاست is the past of 'to rise / arise'.
segments.s156-g29: مهان جهان is a poetic compound 'guests of the world' = 'people/attendees'. جامه کردند چاک is an idiomatic biclausal action: جامه (garments) + کردند (they did) + چاک (torn / rent) — together 'they tore their garments' (a conventional mourning gesture). Word order places the garments before the verb complex.
segments.s156-g30: The clause inverts normal order for poetic effect: به ابر اندر آمد is 'into the cloud(s) came' (به = 'into/towards', اندر = 'in/within', آمد = 'came'), and the subject follows: سر گرد و خاک = 'a head/whirl (سر) of dust (گرد) and dirt (خاک)' — i.e. a great swirling of dust rose up into the clouds.
segments.s156-g31: همه کاخ places the scene 'all through the palace'. تابوت is the noun 'coffin(s)'. بد سر به سر is an idiomatic arrangement: سر به سر = 'head to head' / 'one beside another', and بد here functions adverbially (poetic usage) describing the state/arrangement — literally 'bad/ill' in the lexicon but here contributing the sense 'lying (in that condition)'. The phrase as a whole describes the coffins arranged head-to-head throughout the palace.
segments.s156-g32: غنوده is a past/participial form 'reposed / lay sleeping'. بصندوق is the prepositional compound 'in/into the chest/box' (ب + صندوق). در شیر نر is a locative phrase 'in the (statue/figure or place of the) male lion' (در = 'in', شیر نر = 'male lion') indicating where the chest was located.
segments.s156-g17: همی گفت is a classical/perfective-past iterative construction meaning 'kept saying' or 'was saying' (همی marks continuous/iterative aspect with a past verb). زال is the subject (proper name) and precedes the predicate; اینت is an archaic/poetic demonstrative (essentially 'this' or 'this is') linking to کاری شگفت ('a wondrous deed'). کاری here functions as a noun phrase 'a deed/work' (poetic usage), and شگفت is a modifying adjective 'wonderful, astonishing.'
segments.s156-g18: که introduces a content (relative/subordinate) clause 'that/which...' giving the content of what Zāl was saying. Word order is typical older Persian: subject (سهراب) + object phrase (گرز گران, with the adjective گران postpositive) + verb (برگرفت) at the end — 'Sohrab took up (برگرفت) the heavy (گران) mace (گرز).' برگرفت is simple past 'took up/raised.'
segments.s156-g19: نشانی شد is a predicate where نشانی ('a sign/mark') is linked to شد (become/appeared) — literally 'a sign became' = 'a sign appeared.' اندر میان is a fixed prepositional phrase 'in/among,' and مهان (plural) means 'the guests/assembly' (i.e. those present). The construction places the resulting predicate before the prepositional phrase for emphasis.
segments.s156-g20: نزاید is an older/poetic negative-present form meaning 'is not born/arisen' (ن + زاید from زاییدن/زادیدن). چنو ('چنین' contracted) means 'such' or 'like this' and modifies مادر ('mother'); اندر جهان is the locative adverbial 'in the world.' Word order is verb ('is not born') + modifier + noun + locative: 'such a mother is not born in the world.'
segments.s156-g21: همی گفت again marks iterative/continuous past 'he kept saying.' The coordinate ו ('and') links the next result clause: مژگان پر از آب کرد literally 'he made (کرد) the eyelashes full of water' — a poetic way to say his eyes filled with tears. Note پر از is a postpositive adjective phrase 'full of X' placed before the verb کرد (causative/do).
segments.s156-g22: زبان پر ز گفتار سهراب کرد has the same pattern: زبان ('tongue' used metonymically for 'speech') + پر ز گفتار سهراب ('full of the speech/words of Sohrab') + کرد (causative/performative 'made/caused'). The preposition ز (poetic for از) introduces the content that fills the tongue: 'with/from the speech of Sohrab.' Word order places the affected noun and its modifiers before the verb.
segments.s156-g23: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when he came' (چو = 'when'). In older Persian it is common for the verb آمد to precede the subject: 'چو آمد تهمتن' = 'when Tahmatan came.' به ایوان خویش is the prepositional phrase 'to his own iwan/porch' — خویش is formed from خوی + enclitic ش ('his'), so the possessive is split into those elements in the provided segmentation.
segments.s156-g24: خروشید is an intransitive verb 'he cried/shouted/roared' (a loud exclamation). The conjunction و links the next action: تابوت بنهاد پیش — بنهاد is an archaic past form of نهادن meaning 'set/placed' (here 'placed the coffin'), and پیش functions adverbially 'before/in the presence of' (i.e. placed the coffin down before them). Together the coordinated clause reads 'he cried out and placed the coffin before [them].'
segments.s156-g1: وزان جایگه is a fronted prepositional/conjunctive phrase: وزان (poetic fusion of و + زان) = 'and/from that', followed by جایگه 'place'. شاه is the subject and لشکر the object; براند is the past 3rd-singular of راندن (archaic poetic past with -د) meaning 'drove' or 'led (on)'. Word order in classical Persian often places adverbial/prepositional phrases before the subject and verb.
segments.s156-g2: به ایران خرامید is a prepositional phrase (به + place) + past verb خرامید ('strode, walked proudly'); و links the next clause. In the coordinated clause رستم بماند, رستم is the subject and بماند is past 3rd-singular ('remained'/'was left'); note the classical use of ب- before the verb in some past forms.
segments.s156-g3: بدان here is a fused/discourse-form 'be aware / know this' (ب- as prefix attached to دان 'know'); تا introduces the subordinate clause ('that' / 'until') و زواره بیاید ز راه is the clause 'Zawareh may/comes from the road' with بیاید in the subjunctive/present-future sense. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and راه = 'road/path'.
segments.s156-g4: بدو is the indirect object 'to him'; آگهی آورد is the verbal phrase 'brought news / reported' with آورد past 3rd-singular. زان سپاه is a trailing prepositional phrase 'from that army' (زان = 'from that'). Word order places the indirect object before the verb in this clause.
segments.s156-g5: چو آمد is a temporal clause marker: چو = 'when' + آمد 'came/arrived'. زواره is the place/person-name that is the subject of آمد. سپیده دمان is a poetic time phrase 'at the break of dawn' (سپیده 'dawn' + دمان literally 'hem/edge', used figuratively for 'dawn's edge').
segments.s156-g6: سپه راند is a clause with سپه 'army/host' as subject and راند (past of راندن) 'drove/put to flight' as verb. رستم هم اندر زمان is an appended phrase: رستم 'Rostam', هم 'also', اندر زمان 'at that time / at the moment' — classical word order places time/adverbial phrases after the noun; here it stresses that Rostam too was involved at the same moment.
segments.s156-g7: پس آنگه are consecutive temporal adverbs ('then' / 'thereupon') and سوی زابلستان کشید means 'he drew/moved toward Zabulistan': سوی = 'toward', زابلستان = place, and کشید is the past 3rd-singular of کشیدن. In classical/poetic Persian کشیدن can mean 'to move/advance' (here 'to set out toward'); the lexical list given includes agricultural senses but in poetry کشیدن commonly denotes motion.
segments.s156-g8: چو here again = 'when'; آگاهی از وی = 'news/information about him' (آگاهی + از + وی). به دستان رسید is literal 'reached the hands' (دستان = 'hands'), an idiom meaning 'was received/reached someone'; رسید is past 'reached/arrived'. Possessive is often implied in such constructions.
segments.s156-g49: چنین است = 'it is thus/so' (چنین + copula است). و links the clauses. رازش is راز + possessive -ش 'its secret'. نیامد پدید is a poetic inversion of پدید نیامد ('did not come to light/appear'): نیامد (negated past/present auxiliary) + پدید (appear) together mean 'did not become manifest'.
segments.s156-g50: نیابی is negative second-person present/subjunctive (ن + یابی) meaning 'you will not / cannot find' (poetic use). به + خیره forms an adverbial phrase 'by (means of) staring' where به = 'by' and خیره = 'staring/with fixed gaze'. چه jویی کلید is an interrogative clause: چه 'what' precedes the verb جویی ('you seek/search') and کلید is its object—literally 'what do you seek (the) key for?'.
segments.s156-g51: در بسته = 'closed door' (در here = 'door' and بسته = 'closed'). را is the definite/object marker attached to that phrase. کس نداند = 'no one knows' (کس used with the negative verb expresses 'no one'). گشاد is the verbal adjective/stem meaning 'open' (here functioning like an infinitive/participial 'to open'); the clause order is somewhat inverted in poetic style (object + را before the verb).
segments.s156-g52: بدین is the prepositional phrase 'with/by this' (ب- as the prefix 'به' + دین as the demonstrative 'this' in archaic/poetic spelling). رنج عمر تو = 'the suffering of your life' (رنج = suffering, عمر = lifetime, تو = you). گردد بباد is a compound describing result: گردد (گردیدن used as 'become' / 'will come to be') + بباد ('be blown away' / 'be lost'); together 'will be blown away/lost.'
segments.s156-g53: یکی داستانست = 'it is a certain tale' (یکی as 'a certain', داستانست = 'is a tale' with the copula -ست). پر آب چشم is an adjectival phrase 'full of the water of the eye'—i.e. 'full of tears'—with پر ('full (of)') modifying آب چشم ('eye's water' = tears). Word order places the descriptive phrase after the copula in poetic style.
segments.s156-g54: دل‌نازک = 'tender‑hearted' (noun/adjective). از رستم indicates source/cause or 'on account of Rostam' ('toward Rostam'). آید is third‑person singular present 'comes/arises' (emotion arises). بخشم here is used idiomatically with آید to express that pity/compassion is produced in the speaker; the form corresponds to the root بخشیدن ('to give/bestow'), so the phrase means roughly 'my pity arises' or 'I am moved to give pity.' This is a poetic construction where an emotion 'comes' and the verb for 'bestowing' names that emotion.
segments.s156-g55: برین داستان = 'on/upon this tale' (بر = 'on/upon', ین is the archaic/poetic form of این 'this'). من سخن ساختم = 'I composed the speech/words' with من as an emphatic subject and سخن ساختم ('I built/composed speech') using ساختم (past first person of ساختن) to mean 'I composed.' The object/goal (this tale) is placed before the verb in poetic order.
segments.s156-g56: به کار سیاووش = 'to/for the affair/matter of Siyâvash' (به = 'to/for', کار = 'affair/matter', سیاووش proper name). پرداختم is the past first person of پرداختن 'to attend to / to treat (a topic)'—here 'I treated/devoted (my words) to the story of Siyâvash.' The verb places the poet's action (devoting attention) in the past.

sentences.s156-s5: All of Sistan came forward again; they came with suffering, with pain, and with anguish.
sentences.s156-s6: When the coffin was seen, Sam alighted from his golden steed.
sentences.s156-s7: Tahmatan went forward on foot; his garments were all torn, his heart stricken, and his beard (disordered).
sentences.s156-s8: The ranks opened all along the flank; everyone, before the coffin, bowed their heads to the ground.
sentences.s156-s17: You said that Sam, with his mane and his firmness, was (so); he was stricken with sorrow from the fighting, came in, and fell asleep.
sentences.s156-s18: He wrapped him again in yellow brocade; he fastened (made firm) the coffin's narrow lid.
sentences.s156-s19: He kept saying, 'If I make a golden tomb, I will encircle it with black musk and ornament it with brightness.'
sentences.s156-s20: When I am gone, there will be no one left in my place; otherwise I myself have no opinion other than this.
sentences.s156-s21: Someone made for him a tomb from the hoof of a steed; the whole world, from lamentation, became blind.
sentences.s156-s22: Thus Bahram spoke, well spoken: 'Do not become familiar with the dead.'
sentences.s156-s23: Do not remain here; if you wish to be long-lived, be well prepared and make no delay.
sentences.s156-s24: One day will give you your father's turn; if it befit, when your turn comes upon you (you must) bear it.
sentences.s156-s13: He drew out a nail from it and opened the lid; he took the shroud off it and set it before his father.
sentences.s156-s14: They showed his body to those renowned men; you said that smoke rose from the sky.
sentences.s156-s15: The guests of the world tore their garments; into the clouds came a head/whirl of dust and dirt.
sentences.s156-s16: All through the palace the coffins lay, head to head; they lay reposed in a chest within the male lion.
sentences.s156-s9: Zāl kept saying, "This is a wondrous thing — that Sohrab took up a heavy mace."
sentences.s156-s10: A sign appeared among the guests; such a mother is not born in the world.
sentences.s156-s11: He kept speaking, his eyelashes filling with water, and his tongue became full of speech about Sohrab.
sentences.s156-s12: When Tahmatan came to his iwan (porch), he cried aloud and set the coffin down before them.
sentences.s156-s1: From that place the king drove (off) the army; he strode into Iran, and Rostam remained.
sentences.s156-s2: Know this: until Zawareh comes from the road, that army brought him news.
sentences.s156-s3: When Zawareh arrived at the break of dawn, the army fled; Rostam likewise at that moment ...
sentences.s156-s4: Then he moved toward Zabulistan; when news about him reached (the) hands (i.e., was received).
sentences.s156-s25: It is thus, and its secret did not come to light; you will not find the key by staring.
sentences.s156-s26: No one knows how to open a closed door; with this suffering your life will be blown away.
sentences.s156-s27: It is a tale full of tears; my tender heart is moved to pity for Rostam.
sentences.s156-s28: On this tale I composed my words; I applied them to the affair of Siyâvash.
